name: UK Woodland Photography: A Year-Round Practical Guide

step.name: Pick the shot type before you arrive
step.text: Decide day before. One of four shot types — chaos-tamed wide, single-tree subject, intimate detail/macro, abstract minimalism. Drives lens, arrival time, conditions wanted. Commit and build session around delivering it.

step.name: Check forecast for right conditions
step.text: Met Office 24-48 hours out. Looking for overcast bright, mist, or low-angle directional sun. Avoid bright direct sun under full canopy June-August. Mist forecast: cold clear overnight + damp ground + still wind. Switch shot type if forecast wrong.

step.name: Arrive before conditions peak
step.text: Mist: 30 min before sunrise (burns off in 90-120 min on dry days). Side-lit edges: 60-90 min before peak directional light. Walk woodland on arrival — 15-minute slow scout consistently produces better frames than hurried setups.

step.name: Set up tripod deliberately at chosen viewpoint
step.text: Not full extension by default — lower viewpoints often stronger. Polariser rotated for maximum effect on wet surfaces. Aperture priority f/8-f/11, ISO 100, RAW. Mirror lockup or electronic shutter. Single-point AF on most important plane.

step.name: Run squinting test before each frame
step.text: Bright patches must be excluded by recomposition or wide-margin capture for cropping. Dark masses revealed by squinting are structural elements. 3-5 bracketed exposures per composition. Move on while light still good.

step.name: Cull and edit lightly with restrained tonality
step.text: Cull on import — sharp frames only, exclude bright-sky patches. 4-8 keepers per 90-minute session. Restrained editing — woodland tonality is naturally subtle, heavy contrast/clarity breaks the genre's register. Heavy editing = capture wasn't strong enough.
